comments.title: Demonic influence of divination.
comments.content: If I understand correctly, some commenters in the Covid posts attribute divinations that “approve” of getting vaccinated as being influenced by demons.  How could one protect themselves from demon influence for divinations about other situations?

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content: There are various ways to do it. Some modes of divination seem to be less vulnerable than others -- horary astrology, for example, seems pretty safe. Doing divination inside a space that's been cleansed with a banishing ritual will help; natural magic may also be worth trying, though I don't have any data on that yet. Prayer is always useful as a way to make sure you're getting in contact with the right sources -- there's a good reason it's called divination...

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content: Could it be that these people are assuming demonic influences are at work, rather than being willing to accept a divination result because it wasn't what they wanted to hear?  How best to tell the difference?

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content: When the divination is insisting everything will be fine and you know one person who dropped dead from a heart attack a week after getting the vaccine and four more who are still dealing with nasty long-term health effects from it, there's at least reason for suspicion. In several cases, though, people have noted other signs of demonic influence -- subjective, but noticeable -- and drew the logical conclusion.

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content:
Not a question, but @ JMG, this is a great (seemingly throwaway) reply on divination that, were it to be fully "unpacked" (as the parlance goes these days), could more or less communicate an entire metaphysics...

Axé

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content: It certainly was meant to imply one!

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content:
As a data point, I did a tarot divination a couple of weeks ago immediately after banishing in my usual space and prayer for a protected reading. It came back as overwhelmingly positive. The eventual outcome being the Ten of Cups. It was a very utopian reading. The first card drawn though was one that has come up a lot for me in recent weeks during my daily draw and it stuck out to me, possibly indicating that there was something wrong with the reading. I took it as the divine saying be careful with this reading.

Even without the discussion here in recent weeks about divination being potentially influenced, I would have been skeptical of that reading. I wish I hadn't done the reading because I have become more skeptical of every reading I've done since then.

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content: Interesting. Okay, that's well worth knowing.

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content:
You know, in the context of horary astrology possibly being less vulnerable to manipulation, I think I should bring up (possibly bring up again, I may have mentioned this before?) a data point on a slightly different and older subject.

Last year, when I saw a bunch of people reporting horary charts indicating that Trump would win reelection, I tried casting my own to see if I could reproduce this. I wound up casting three charts in all - I unfortunately forgot to write down what the second one's question was, but the first was "will Trump win reelection?" and the third was "will Trump win [state I was in]?"

I was, in the end, unable to corroborate the other astrologers' results, but the interesting thing is why this was the case: in each case the chart I cast had at least one of Lilly's significators of a chart unfit to judge. (And, again, three charts, so by the third I was in enemy action territory.)

Not sure what was going on here (it *could* just be that horary is not my specialty, but then my results seem to have been more applicable than the more experienced astrologers' results!), but it seems potentially noteworthy enough that I'm bringing it up. (Perhaps I should cast some horary charts for the pandemic and the vaccines going forwards and see if the same thing happens...)

- pretentious_username

replies.title: Re: Demonic influence of divination.
replies.content: That makes a great deal of sense -- many thanks for the data point.

comments.title: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
comments.content:
Dear JMG (or Papa G as ErikaLopez calls you),

Firstly, thanks very much for this forum and your other & previous ones. I have learned so much here! I have some questions about a series of divinations and about a dream. I know you don't practice dream interpretation but perhaps your other readers could chime in.

Some background: I sensed a disconnect from my then-girlfriend while on an out-of-town work trip. Sure enough, when I returned and talked to her, she said "we have to talk." I was dumped, not for another man, but because "we're different". True enough, mainly class differences and some divergence in interests, but it never bothered me, and I never thought it was realistic to expect someone with an exact overlap of interests. In fact, I was quite in love with & devoted to her and was looking forward to doting on her and taking care of her in our old age (we're both in our 50s). The past year has kept us apart due to COVID and her family, but we did enjoy our time together. She dumped me when kids moved out and she could finally spend significant time with me and develop some interests together - I think the impending reality of that situation may have frightened her a bit and pushed her to her sudden decision.
She had broken with me once before, and I sensed she would come back, to the extent that I felt her presence in bed next to me while away from her. This time, I don't get that same comforting sense, but I still feel a tie or bond to her - things still unresolved. I am deeply wounded and hurt by her decision, and at times very desiring of getting back together with her. I've had good luck with my Marseilles deck before, although not always on target, so I decided to do a reading.

Reconstructing these from memory, the first reading (three-card spread, representing past, present, and future) was a direct question of whether I would get back with her. I don't remember all the cards, but the world card made an appearance representing what I lost, but the final card was the two of cups. The two of cups hit me like a thunderbolt - that seemed to indicate that I would end up back with her. While I wanted this very much, I was very suspicious that it seemed to accord with my desire. I decided to do another reading the next day, just on the more general question of whether I would find romantic love in the future. This time, the final card was the one of cups. At this point I began to think that I was doing some kind of wish-fulfillment through the tarot. Finally, running through these portents in my mind over and over again, I kept hearing the mantra "the cards don't lie." So, on the third day, I did a reading on the same question and got the two of coins as the future. This I read as a future in turmoil, but I realized I had drawn the cards before formulating my question, so I decided to do one more spread, this time being careful to formulate my question before drawing the cards. As I was picking the last card, a voice said - "that's it, that's the card." It was the two of cups. The world card also made an appearance in this spread. This is after carefully shuffling the cards three times after each reading, reversing half the deck between each shuffle, and cutting the deck at the end.

I was skeptical about these results, so after two days I did one more spread to ask if I was fooling myself through the tarot. This spread I thought of as case, opposing arguments, and judgement. The third card was the magician, reversed. I realized immediately that that was a stupid question, and took that card as an ambiguous result - perhaps yes I am, perhaps an "frack-you for asking".

I also prayed and asked for signs and dreams to guide me. I had a brief dream in which she was rummaging through my mind, opening drawers and cabinets, looking frantically for something. I was scared of the image of her monkeying in my head and woke up immediately.

So, the questions:

1. Is it possible I could have fooled myself with the tarot on the first three readings, getting wish fulfillment instead of real readings, given my intense feelings on this subject?

2. How should I interpret the fourth reading? Should I set it aside?

3. Dream interpretations?

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content:
1) It's quite easy to fool yourself with most divination methods, especially when you have strong emotions about something. It's been standard advice for some time to get someone else to do the reading for you in that situation.

2) The tarot doesn't like to be pestered with repeated questions on the same subject. It's quite notorious for that.

3) Dream interpretation is something you have to have a gift for, and I don't. Sorry.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content:
I don't consider myself gifted in dream interpretation so take my advice with as much salt as you like.

Does the ex have any interest in magic? If yes, ask someone here who does free divinations if she's messing with you magically (or otherwise).

If no, then write out as many possible interpretations as you can, do any resonate? (Make you feel something?) Pay special attention to those. I can't tell you much past that because I don't know what you want to get out of it.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content:
No, my ex is very much a normie, so it’s not her messing with me in my dream.

I’ll see if I can get someone else to do a reading for me.

Thank you both!

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content:
I do dream interpretation.

Since finding out whether you'll get back together is effectively asking something that requires knowing her intentions and feelings as well, what would asking your question over and over again effectively be trying to do to her?

Look carefully at the spread you got before you were sent that dream to clarify.

You got your answers, your job now is to wait. If they're right, it's on her to initiate, she knows how you feel.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content: I've been interpreting my own dreams for a couple years and occasionally I'll interpret for my immediate family.  If I had this dream I would think that there is some aspect of my psyche that my girlfriend wants me to have in order for us to be in a relationship.  She knows I have it but can't find it, so she broke up with me. YMMV.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content: See comment below to Blackwulf - I probably was more committed than she was.  Sigh.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content: Do you have any children together or were the children that left all hers?  I believe your dream is your emotional state, you are feeling frantic and time is running out. What you are looking for is what attracted her to you in the first place, something you had maybe put away.  She is probably searching for that too.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Reading on Romantic Trouble
replies.content: We both have kids, but not with each other.  She might have been looking for more attention - I have been pulled in many directions with work and my kids as of late.  I was extremely patient with her when her family duties took her away, but perhaps I saw a commitment that she obviously didn't share.

comments.title: The Room of Mirrors
comments.content:
Good Evening JMG!

I took to an experiment an experiment with my mind.

Once I learned to meditate constructively, divine and read about the mind as spoken on some of the traditional occultism literature I found myself with a mental image which I've called the Room of Mirrors. This springs out of a frustration on failing to change the things that flow through my mind, since repeatedly I've found myself looking at things with the same perspective just jumbled up in a different drag; facing the same situations with the same problems. One thing however has helped me to make different things flow through my mind and that has been talking to different people and visiting different places.

Oddly enough, when I meditate on a place I can tell that the flavor of the thoughts are different than the ones in my room and when I meditate on a social gathering (yes, I did this in the bathroom!) another set of very different thoughts flow through mind. Both these experiences gave me a fresh and new perspective into how I see my life. Sometimes overstating some of colors which I see it with, lighting up some of the things I couldn't see and also understating some of the things that I usually make a remark on. Picturing this as lense, the eye being my mind it is as if the light bounces in different ways and colors depending on the lense --because somehow I get this bouncing feeling, it's not a straight stream of thoughts, but rather a path, sometimes crooked and sometimes curvileanear (like the shape of a river). This I also experience when I talk to someone in person, suddendly an extra stream that wasn't there before is there, flowing into my perception.

With this in mind I've created a set of doors, each with a sigil made by letting my hand move in a sort of brownian motion while thinking intently about the "current" I want to visit and then refining the design by thinking about it a little more to make it easier to remember and more stylized to the eye. It seems to work, though it is definitely not the same as sitting on a riverside or a mountain, but it works to some extent.

A few questions:
1) Is there any consideration that I should take while exploring this room of mirrors? What do you think about it?

2) Before I started this experiment and somehow as an inspiration for it, once my mediation practice took roots into my daily routine, I started to feel a little bit of confusion about just what the heck is real. My mind and conceptions of the world flow in different ways, I can control my moods just by an act of will. More generally the way I experiment the world through my senses is vastly different from the one my mind assembles from those inputs. I assume this sense of confusion is related to the sense of loosing what I thought to be a hard and solid truth of experience of the world. This can be a little disorienting at times. Common enough to not worry about it?

3) Lastly, my daily magical routine looks like this. LRP (between 10-15 minutes), relaxing into possition (5 minutes), pore breathing (5-10 minutes), meditation (15-20 minutes), Natural breath (5 minutes). Something that I noticed is that I've ended up more than once sitting or standing in a sort of meditation mood seeing pictures and thoughts in my secondary vision. I assume it is because of the mental training. Am I correct that doing physical exercise would ground my energies back again into my physical body?

replies.title: Re: The Room of Mirrors
replies.content:
1) That kind of approach is one that many people find very useful.

2) It's very common. Don't worry about it.

3) Yes. Another thing many occult writers recommend is taking up some kind of handicraft, which also helps refocus attention on the material plane.

comments.title: Tarot divination exercises in LRM
comments.content:
Dear John,

I am on the fifteenth lesson of LRM, and have some questions about Tarot divination and Tarot cards in general.

1. I have not been getting meaningful results from the divination exercises in lessons 14 and 15. I used to keep the cards without any protection (silk or linen) for quite a while, in the same room I do the LBRP/MP (I have fixed this since getting to know about the effect of LBRP/MP on the cards) - could this be the reason for the lack of relatable/meaningful divinations?

2. I have also been doing the Levi book reading, and have printed/laminated the Marseille trumps. These cards are also stored covered in the same (silk) cloth - is this required, or can they be stored separately, without any protection? Also, could the Marseille trumps be 'interfering' with the main deck? I have recently started separating the main deck from these Trumps by intervening layers of cloth.

3. When trying to interpret the cards for the divination, should I use the words I had come up with for each number card (as part of the earlier exercises), or should I interpret each card afresh?

Apologies if I have missed the answers to these questions provided elsewhere.

Many thanks for this forum and for your answers.

replies.title: Re: Tarot divination exercises in LRM
replies.content:
1 and 2) Neither of those are likely. The cards aren't consecrated and therefore can't be deconsecrated by banishing rituals. As for interference, I keep the decks I used most side by side on a shelf, and have never had any trouble. You might simply be dealing with inexperience -- tarot divination isn't easy.

3) Try it both ways and see which one gives you better results. There is no fixed rule.

comments.title: Monsters in the Dark, Tree First Aid, Elemental Gate Imbalance, Church of the Subgenius
comments.content:
Good evening and a happy early Alban Elued to you! May the fruits of the past year sustain you through coming winter and become the seeds for new growth in the spring.

A few more serious/immediately practical questions, and a couple that are pure curiosity, listed roughly in order of how much I'd appreciate your response:

1. My daughter has recently started being worried about monsters in the dark (she's not quite three, so it's about the right age for that to be starting). I don't want to fall into the trap of telling her "they're not real", since I would like her to keep a healthy respect for her imagination. Also, since learning more about the occult, I now have to take seriously the idea that it's not a given that what's scaring her is entirely within her head. Any thoughts on how best to help her deal with her fears and how concerned to be about actual malevolent entities? Precautions already taken: there's currently one of the red bag talismans from the FAQ under her mattress at the end where she puts her head, I do a daily SOP (but not around her or where she spends much time), I pray on her behalf, and I give regular offerings to the spirits of our house and land.

2. They're building a house next door, and in the process of putting in a drain pipe from the backyard, they cut through the roots of three mature (~70 years old) oaks near the property line. Anything I can do to help them cope with the damage besides taking a hoodoo bath and pouring the water on them?

3. I'm working my way through the DMH, and I'm up to learning the Gate of Fire in the SOP. Pretty much since starting the Fire Gate, I've noticed a significantly stronger feeling when I ask for the blessings of Fire than I get from Air or Water, despite having practiced each of those for at least 3 weeks before moving forward. Is this an imbalance I should work to correct, something interesting to keep an eye on, or just a normal part of the process?

4. You've mentioned before that there's some uncertainty about when the Age of Aquarius actually began - what are the sources of the uncertainty? Is it a difference between sidereal and tropical astrology? Different judgments of how wide a swath of the sky counts as "Aquarius"? Using different days of the year to watch where the sun is rising? With what I understand about precession, and if the same date of the spring equinox and the same 30 degree chunks of sky were used, I'm not understanding where the room for interpretation is left.

5. Any thoughts on the Church of the Subgenius?

As always, thanks very much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Monsters in the Dark, Tree First Aid, Elemental Gate Imbalance, Church of the Subgenius
replies.content:
1) It's very unlikely that your daughter will actually encounter any malign entities, and if you've got a protective charm in place that risk drops very close to zero. One thing that some parents in my circle of readers have done, however, is to teach their children Abby Judson's etheric banishing exercise. It helps keep their energies clean and does them no harm at all.

2) You can always go talk to them and listen for a resppnse.

3) That's quite common. Most people attune more strongly with one of the elements than with the others.

4) The gods neglected to draw boundary lines in the sky, so we don't know where the lines between the constellations are. The 30° wedges move along with the precession, since they're defined by the solstices and equinoxes!

5) A fine bit of silliness with occasional bursts of genuine wisdom. Praise Bob!

replies.title: Re: Monsters in the Dark, Tree First Aid, Elemental Gate Imbalance, Church of the Subgenius
replies.content:
Thanks much for all the answers.

On 4) - Ahah! Duh, that should have been obvious with a bit more reflection. As someone prone to our age's preference for abstraction, I am still learning to ground intellectual systems in tangible reality, and gaps of understanding like this are likely more common than I realize. Thanks very much for the correction. For your own interpretation of the beginning of the age, is there a source that you follow, or have you arrived at it through your own observation and judgment?

replies.title: Re: Monsters in the Dark, Tree First Aid, Elemental Gate Imbalance, Church of the Subgenius
replies.content: I'm still exploring possibilities. Something certainly shifted around 1880, which is when a lot of the late 19th and early 20th century occultists thought the Aquarian Age began -- a whole truckload of Uranian-Aquarian influences surged up, and a great many Neptunian-Piscean influences began to fade out in a big way. But there's another tradition, much older, which claims that the age begins with the first grand conjunction in the sign that rules it -- in which case the Age of Aquarius began late last year...

replies.title: Re: Monsters in the Dark, Tree First Aid, Elemental Gate Imbalance, Church of the Subgenius
replies.content: That's fascinating, and seems so plausible. Can you say any more about the older tradition?

replies.content: I'm still chasing down sources. I caught some details of it in Hamlet's Mill, but I'm far from sure where the authors thereof got it.

replies.content:
Hmm. I've been thinking about this too. Just got "Hamlet's Mill" in but I don't know when I'll have time to read it yet.

I'd be interested in the sources for the older tradition (for both actually) as it seems like there was quite a shift late last year... it's hard to tell with such big cycles though. I think it was Arnold Toynbee who called the period right around then "The Mediterranean world's age of agony," and things weren't much better on the other side of Eurasia -- that was the final, cataclysmic stage of the era of Warring States. So I think the answer would be "yes"...

replies.content: Hi jprussel. We don't have children but I was a kid once. I remember a few things from that very young period. One was being afraid there was something in my closet and wanting the door completely closed. From my bed I could see the door but not into the dark area of the closet where the door was partly open. Was also afraid that something under my bed could 'get me', maybe reach up or something. I needed by feet and arms covered with the blanket for protection. replies.title: Re: Monsters in the Dark, Tree First Aid, Elemental Gate Imbalance, Church of the Subgenius
replies.content:
omg. May I just say that is the sweetest story ever. Your mom was a boss.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) I'm sorry to say that's not a subject I know anything noticeably about.

2) Schopenhauer thought that his philosophy was a unity, the unfolding of a single concept. It wasn't -- it consisted of a concept plus a value judgment. He himself admitted that it would be possible for someone to affirm the goodness of the will --

"A man who had thoroughly assimilated the truths we have already advanced, but had not come to know, either from his own experience or from a deeper insight, that constant suffering is essential to life, who found satisfaction and all that he wished in life, and could calmly and deliberately desire that his life, as he had hitherto known it, should endure for ever or repeat itself ever anew, and whose love of life was so great that he willingly and gladly accepted all the hardships and miseries to which it is exposed for the sake of its pleasures,—such a man would stand 'with firm-knit bones on the well-rounded, enduring earth,' and would have nothing to fear."

-- It was just that couldn't do it. Thus his condemnation of the world need not be accepted by those who accept his concept but not his value judgment. Thus the quote you cite doesn't concern me -- it's an attempt to drag Schopenhauer back into the Christianity he loathed.

3) As for godhead, the Will is not a god. It's an oddity of the Abrahamic faiths to see the source of all things as a divinity, rather than a creative chaos from which deities, along with all other things, are born.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
For what it's worth, my view is that such a tradition either did exist, or could exist-- but it will take some work to bring it into being.

In the Chinese system, the hours are not equivalent to hours of the clock. Rather, daytime and nighttime are each divided into 12 equal hours, marked by the setting and rising of the Sun. Sunrise and Sunset are both marked as 6:00, am or pm as the case may be-- *regardless of what your actual clock says.* That latter point is critical-- at the present time, with the Equinox just 2 days away, the day and night are roughly equal length and so an "hour" is actually 60 minutes long. But sunrise is currently around 7:00.

That means that, for right now, Lung Time is actually around 4am to 6am. Large Intestine Time begins at 6AM and goes until 8AM. All of this will change by a whole hour at Daylight Savings Time. It will also slowly change as we approach the Winter Solstice-- depending on where you are, a Winter Night Hour can be very long (it will be around 75 minutes in my area at the Winter Solstice) and a Winter Day Hour very short (45 minutes or so, around here).

So that point's critical if you want to use the Chinese clock. In your case, it's very possible that you're actually waking up during Liver Time instead of Lung Time-- and that's very common, actually, as the Liver is associated with the part of the soul that journeys abroad during sleep (the Hun or ethereal soul.)

Now, the reason I wanted to talk about the details of the Chinese system is that it points out how the Western equivalent might work. We have a system of 12 hours which works in the same way as the Chinese-- it's the system of Planetary Hours. Look this up if you're unfamiliar-- Christopher Warnock discusses it here: https://www.renaissanceastrology.com/planetaryhoursarticle.html

Traditionally, each planet rules parts of the body. The signs, too, are given rulership over the body and various health issues. It's almost certainly the case that a system of either planetary rulerships, or sign rulerships, could be worked out, and then either this set of hours or (more likely) some variation on it could be (re-)created. It would take a lot of work and testing, though.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm not sure how much any of these cover change over time, but here's the books on Greek and Roman religion I held onto from being a Classics major in college:

- Greek Religion by Walter Burkert
- Ancient Greek Religion by Mikalson
- Religions of the Ancient Greeks by Price
- Religions of Rome - Volume 1: A History by Beard, North, and Price
- Sources for the Study of Greek Religion by Rice & Stambaugh
- Arcana Mundi: Magic and the Occult in the Greek and Roman Worlds - A Collection of Ancient Texts by Georg Luck
- Witchcraft and Magic in Europe: Ancient Greece and Rome - Edited by Ankarloo and Clark

Aside from the books, a potentially helpful source to get a broader view of changes in beliefs about the gods might be to look into comparative Indo-European mythology - looking for what's the same/different between the Greeks, Romans, Norse, Persians, Hindus and so forth. This will tend to give you an idea about what was earliest.

You can then move forward a bit by learning about what little we know about Mycenean religion, get another snapshot by reading Homer, still another reading the plays and philosophers of classical Athens, still another from the Hellenistic period, and then the Roman empire and late antiquity.

There's not as much on Roman religion as distinct from Greek, but there are some tantalizing glimpses of Etruscan religion and its influence on the early Romans (some of those influences persisted quite a long time), and potentially more glimpses of practice rather than myth, thanks to extensive records and the integration with the government.

It also occurs to me that readoldthings might have some helpful thoughts here, as he provided a very comprehensive recommendation on how to approach Neoplatonic thought of late antiquity.

One can't really have a good conversation under such circumstances unless you're truly open to the possibility that the other party's reasons for doing what he does might turn out to be weightier than your reasons for objecting to it--in other words, that he's just as much within his rights to do it as you are within yours to object to it, or more so, and that you really don't have any sort of absolute right on your side.

So ... please imagine how a farmer might feel who has farmed his inherited farmland all his life, with pigs and cows and goats, etc. And then the price of farmlaand goes up, and neighboring farmers sell out, and many houses are built ... and his new neighbors are suburbanites who were promised a suburb by the realtors, and who just hate all the smells and noises from his animals, and the engine roar and gas fumes from his tractor and his off-road truck, and the way that the manure pile obtrudes on their view, and the very early start that the farmer gets on his chores, and ... and ... and ...

And then one of his neighbors starts to go on to him about pollution and cow-produced methane and vegetarian diets and all sorts of other things that actually make ordinary farmers out to be the bad guys, and the ways of farming out to be evil ways.

Frankly, my sympathies are all with the farmer here.

However, the real villains in this scenario are not the farmer's new neighbors, but the wretched developers and vile realtors and pond-scum mortgage sellers who are making lots and lots of money by repurposing good farmland and exploiting the folk who just want to buy a better house in a prettier and cleaner environment. They are doing far more harm to the planet than any amount of cows with their methane.

comments.title: Gods on the Mind
comments.content:
I am reading Mysticism by Evelyn Underhill and find myself unexpectedly torn and disappointed. This might be my presumptions regarding mysticism as quite passive, so I'm trying to hold salt in one hand while I read this.

What I want is to be a mage, but to link my mental egregore with the gods. It sounds like that isn't an option, though. The power I want from being a mage I plan to direct inward to help my spiritual advancement.

It feels like I *have* achieved some kind of upgrade in my mental/emotional state though. For several weeks now, I cannot tolerate reading books in my own genre. I feel the park nearby calling to me, a very tangible yearning to go walk and smell the trees with a relaxed mind, by myself.

It feels like changing gears on a manual car, the lower ones being denser, with less consciousness. Right now I'm in a high gear, but I'm wasting gas by trying to gun it, pushing the pedal unskillfully.

My moods and mental fatalisms do not sink me as far, I might dip but I rise back up again. I stay in the higher gears.

Is this changing gears in my consciousness, that feels very tangible, not a linking up with the gods? What is it, then? Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Gods on the Mind
replies.content: Underhill's book is a highly personal one. Her take on mystic experiences--there are several equally "real" kinds, not just one single one--always struck me as basically privileging Western European (and Christian-influenced) views over any broader investigation of the full range of such experiences across all the world's cultures and languages. Jordan Paper has an interesting (and somewhat broader) approach to it in his The Mystic Experience. (This is the same Jordan Paper who wrote The Deities Are Many, which I mentioned here not long ago.)

replies.title: Re: Gods on the Mind
replies.content: I like to cross-reference Underhill's book with Bucke's Cosmic Consciousness, which comes out of the same era but has very different assumptions and conclusions! Those two plus James's The Varieties of Religious Experience (another work of the same period) were the books recommended to me by my teacher John Gilbert when I was studying the work of the Universal Gnostic Church; if it's ever possible to revive that, they'll be the basic texts for training in the Minor Orders.

replies.title: Re: Gods on the Mind
replies.content: Buck's and James' books are very worth-while reads.  I second our host's recommendation.

replies.title: Re: Gods on the Mind
replies.content:
1) Magic and mysticism are two different things. What you're interested in -- practicing magic in relation to the gods, and becoming a vessel for their power and wisdom -- is called theurgy, and it's decidedly different from mysticism. On the other hand, reading the occasional work of mystical literature can have the kind of upgrade effect you've described! You don't have to follow Underhill's vision to benefit from her insights in that way.

2) The shift in consciousness could be any number of things. Meditate, reflect, take that walk in the park, and see what the experience has to teach you.

Hi JMG,

I wanted to ask about a sentiment I've come up several times in these discussions - that the Internet is a world devoid of spirit, or that what spirit it has is entirely demonic.

Perhaps I'm biased because I've spend my life working in technology but the computer world definitely has a spiritual side to it. Certain electronic "places" have an etheric resonance. They can manifest unique effects that I haven't experienced elsewhere, which I suspect is because of the ability of the electronic world to connect people in different locations, to be at the same time subtle and span the globe. In some cyberspaces I've felt spirits moving the same way they do in the natural world, and in computers there's an interplay of order and chaos that can be much like nature.

However, from my perspective the most spiritually resonant era of the electronic world was long ago from the 70s to the 90s. People's attitudes toward computers then were different as well; the machine was seen almost like a shrine, the monitor a window on another world of endless possibilities. There was a reverence and awe for the possibilities that computers represented. Today, while computers are millions of times more powerful, the sense of wonder surrounding them seems greatly diminished. Comparing online spaces like Facebook and Reddit to the communities of yesteryear, it's like coming back to your hometown and finding that the grove near your house has been clearcut, paved over and had a strip mall built over it. And many of the conglomerates that paved over the communities of yesteryear certainly have a demonic cast about them.

Do you think the electronic world is spiritually inert? Can "nature" exist in an artificial world? My intuition has always been that the electronic world has been half created and half discovered, that computers are a contact point with a plane that operates by different laws than our own.

As a magician and a software developer, I have some thoughts on this:

The internet isn't really a "thing" in the conventional sense, rather it is an unimaginably vast set of connections tying together a very large number of very physical tools. In my mind, it is devoid of spirit in the same way that a math textbook is: spirit is not native to the form, but it can easily form some very powerful connections, including with higher realms.

The internet, in my mind, is a VERY VERY mixed bag. It has succeeded marvelously in exactly what it was designed to do: spreading information and making connections. The problems unfold from the fact that many of those connections involve things that most sane people wouldn't really want to be connected to, and degraded information is generally the most popular.

Your intuition about half-discovered and half-invented aligns well with my own views on the subject. Mental objects are capable of condensing down into very powerful and concrete forms using computers. The problem is that it would be better for humanity to rise up to that level of being, not mercilessly drag it down to such a crass level. This may also explain why it has become so corrupted over the last few decades.

On artwork, its is worth remembering that old pixel-art was basically hand created by an artist. It was an odd medium, but it was very much art in the traditional sense. A datapoint I have noticed recently - I moved some testing and staging environments for the work I do from the 'cloud' (servers in other countries) to a purpose built server sitting in the room next to where I work.

For some reason that I cannot articulate, the experience of working with the local system is a whole lot more 'grounded', for lack of a better word. Perhaps it is just the substantially fewer layers of abstraction, or maybe it is just the slightly faster response.

But I don't think so as neither of those changes explain the fundamentally different feeling of using the local system - it is the same software and the same interfaces, but oh so much more relaxing. I was (and still am) genuinely shocked at the difference. It does remind me strongly of when I first started working with computers in the mid-90s.

Perhaps the issue with the internet is not so much the devices or computing itself, but the dislocation of it all. The (popular definition) 'magic' of the internet covers over a vast array of abstractions and gimmicks the give a surface impression of a unified system, but it is really anything but. And that underlying chaos and stress perhaps comes through in ways that are not consciously perceivable.

I remember said healer saying that through the connections between family members it is possible that if one does a step forward and starts resolving his or her problems, it will effect the other family members, sometimes even without direct physical contact. replies.title: Re: Memory
replies.content: The thing to keep in mind here is that you have several different memories. There's your soul's memory, which persists from life to life and is highly accurate, but which most people can't access; there's your body's memory, which is stored in chemical form in the brain, which is relatively accurate in terms of what you yourself experience, but which most people can only access in part; and there's your personality's memory, which is software rather than hardware and is extremely fallible. This latter memory not only can be wrong, much of the time it's at least partly wrong. How many times have you been sure of a fact, only to find out that you misremembered it?

comments.title: Healing ritutal recommendation

Dear JMG,

I’m nearing the end of the ovate grade in the Celtic Golden Dawn curriculum, and I have a question for you: Can you recommend a healing ritual, or a resource where I might find one? I recently meditated on a problem I’ve been having, and it came to me that the solution might be to perform this healing ritual. Left Witness = PEN Y DRAIGG
Right Witness = LLAWENYDD
JUDGE = FFORDD

The problem I meditated on: I was once a trauma victim and suffered many years of PTSD as a result. I’ve done a long course of talk therapy, and while that was beneficial in many ways, I eventually began to feel that there was no way to bring it to a satisfying close, and to continue only seemed to keep me in “victim” mode. The 20th anniversary of 9/11 strikes me as an interesting model. We have memorials all over this country, and on that day, ceremonies were held to remember the tragedy and its survivors. I have no memorial, no ritual to honor and acknowledge my tragedy and loss, and I think it would help if I did.

replies.title: Re: Norse Resource
replies.content:
Heathen is an Old English word with a very similar origin-story as "Pagan" - it means "people of the heath", which means the countryside, where old beliefs persisted. It was coined by Christians to describe those folks who had not converted, whether in the past or contemporary, and it was very pejorative originally. These days, a lot of folks like to use it to self-describe Germanic religion.

There's apparently some controversy and politics over what terminology to use among folks who pray to Germanic Gods. First off, some folks pray to the Norse Gods, while others use the names of Gods from other ancient Germanic cultures (like the Anglo-Saxons or Frisians) - and there's debate over to what degree these are the "same" Gods with different names, different aspects, or what. So, some folks say "Heathen" and mean "anyone worshipping Germanic Gods", while others have a narrower meaning (and, of course, some use it in the old Christian sense of "bad non-Christian"!)

The only reason your father should care about any of this is if he decides to engage with local groups or other folks who practice Germanic religion on social media - some of them will have very definite ideas about what "heathen" does and doesn't mean, and might care a lot about it!

Oh, one other thing to watch out for if he does seek out other heathens/pagans/polytheists/[insert preferred label here] - one major split among believers is "folkishness". Some people believe that the Germanic religions are the indigenous religion of the folks of northern Europe and that being descended from those people is an important part of the religion. Others see that as thinly-veiled racism. The fact that some white supremacists have enthusiastically adopted some Norse symbolism and the old association with the Nazis adds fuel to that particular fire. All of the best sources I have found have managed to navigate this difference in belief carefully and with nuance, whichever side of it they come down on, but that is not exactly what I would expect from a Facebook group or subreddit, so your relative should look out if he ventures to such uncivilized places!

replies.title: Re: Norse Resource
replies.content:
As someone with a longtime interest in Germanic mythology but who is still new to taking religion featuring its Gods seriously, I hope I can offer some help here:

- Mythologically, the primary texts to start with are the Prose and Poetic Edda. I would likely recommend the Prose Edda first, as it is somewhat "tidier" and more systematic than the Poetic Edda, and therefore a little easier to get used to

- Norse Mythology for Smart People is a great guide to that Mythology and makes for a handy reference: https://norse-mythology.org/

- Germanic Mythology is a far more extensive website, but is less helpful as a simple reference. It has a lot of translations of primary texts for free: http://www.germanicmythology.com/

- Getting more into the religion, A Modern Guide to Heathenry by Galina Krasskova is my current favorite "getting started with Germanic Religion" book. The book she co-authored with Raven Kaldera, Northern Tradition for the Solitary Practitioner, gives a lot of specific recommendations on different religious/devotional practices that don't rely on having co-religionists around

- I was pointed to Kveldulf Gundarsson's books by another commenter here and found his guided meditations on the runes extremely helpful (it was during one of those that I had my first "whoah!" religious experience). If your relative is not interested in the runes, I'd go with Teutonic Religion over Teutonic Magic

- As for practices, I have been following JMG's advice to build a foundation of Ritual, Meditation, and Divination. I've also added in some prayer practice

- Ritual: The Heathen Golden Dawn work gullindagan has picked up was my first ritual work, which was working really well, but with some divine guidance, I've taken up our host's Druid Magic Handbook, which I have found works quite well with Norse deities. See JMG's suggestion here for which ones to call upon: https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/59968.html?thread=4929344#cmt4929344

- Meditation: the Eddas work marvelously as themes for meditation. I very productively meditated on the Voluspa one stanza at a time, for example

- Divination: the runes are the clear choice here. Edred Thorsson's Runecaster's Handbook is likely the most useful for straightforward divination, but there are a lot of books on the subject, and I've been into this longer than I've taken the rest seriously, so happy to expand if that's helpful. That being said, I seem to be doing fine learning the Ogham with Odin and Freyja's help, so other forms of divination that speak to him more might work fine as well.

- Prayer: I have benefited from JMG's advice to approach the Gods as extremely wise elders worthy of respect, and to talk to them pretty normally from there. I have also found it helpful to have something a little more structured.

- A good basic framework for a prayer to the Norse Gods that I adapted from other sources goes something like this: Fill a cup with a drink you enjoy. Alcohol is traditional, but if your relative is avoiding that, then juice, coffee, or tea can all work, as can water. Hold up the cup and say, "Hail [God], [one or more epithets]! Thank you for [thing you got]/I ask for [thing wanted]. I give this drink to you in thanks/in worship to you, and in the name of my forebearers who [did something like what you are asking for] I toast!" At this point hold the cup up in a toast, then take a small drink. Dip a finger in the drink and sprinkle a bit of it on yourself (I trace Thor's Hammer on my forehead). If you have an altar, leave the drink there for a bit, otherwise, you can pour it out down the drain or outside.

Here's a specific example for Odin (who in my experience prefers coffee to tea, if I'm not using alcohol): "Hail Odin, wise wayfarer, one-eyed wanderer, husband of Frig and father of Thor! I give this drink to you in thanks for your wisdom and rede (counsel). In the name of my forefathers who sought hidden lore and did well with it, I toast."

replies.title: Re: Norse Resource
replies.content:
He can start with audiobooks of Norse mythological stories to get a feel for the personalities of the pantheon. Audiobooks really helped me through some rough times when I was in pain and having a difficult time concentrating.

If he wants to take it further there is the Encyclopedia of Norse and Germanic Folklore, Mythology, and Magic by Claude Lecouteux. It's a great bathroom reader and also a reference guide for dipping ones' toe into lesser known stories and concepts. Worth it for the bibliography alone.

I have no experience with Asatru as an organized religion, but if it's anything like the rest then expect a mixed bag.

replies.title: Re: Daily divination
replies.content:
I haven't used the runes in a while, but I am seriously thinking about getting back into them.

0) When I was practicing with them regularly, I found them to be highly recursive when the three rune draw was used. Larger and more complicated patterns, in my experience, where much more directed and specific. It sounds like you are doing great work!

1) This is actually a really important insight you are catching onto, and probably close to the heart of the idea of sacrifice. Both runes and geomancy have promised me difficult and painful results from a course of action that I later took; they were right of course, but there was another half to the involved figures. I don't know if this same pattern holds for astrology though, every system has its own temperament and quirks.

2) If you have more time to put into it, there are a number of steps you can take. Making more of an effort to prepare for the divination can drastically improve the results. Paying attention to the "feel" of each rune as you draw it is another step that you can take. Finally, spending some time trying to open your mind to each of the drawn pieces and then to the connections between them is yet another step that can be added.

Good luck, and I hope this is helpful.

-TR

replies.title: Re: Daily divination

With practice, you can learn to tell when the oracle is saying "it's going to be tough, but you can push on through" and when the oracle is saying "no effort of yours can change this." As for the inauguration chart, yes, in theory, a strong, smart, and charismatic president whose inauguration had those omens might be able to get through the crisis without disaster. Since we don't have one of those, I don't expect this to happen.

2) I've always stuck with a simple daily divination, and then added in other divinations, as complex as seems appropriate, to expand things.

comments.title: Physical culture
comments.content:
By any chance have you noticed any synergystic effects from regular practice of the five rites along with regular maxalding exercises?

Also, does it help to visualize each rotating vortex?

replies.title: Re: Physical culture
replies.content:
1) No, I haven't, but your mileage may vary.

2) Yes, it does. It's more important to do the movements as exactly as possible, but once you can do that without having to put all your attention into it, be aware of the vortices and feel them spin, and that'll boost the effect gently.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG and commentariat- hopefully you can help me on a couple of questions about a Tarot reading regarding a possible relationship. I am an inexperienced Tarot reader, and have been experimenting lately with relying much less on "accepted" card meanings which often confuse me more than they enlighten and over-involve the linear thinking mind, and more on meditation on the cards and trying to develop better intuition. So my current ignorance about the actual symbolism on the cards might well stand out like a sore thumb! Here goes:
In a three card spread with the form 1.present situation
2.suggested course of action.
3.likely outcome
..and the question being: Cards, please tell me about me about my connection to this particular woman, the chances of my having a relationship with her, and any way I could possibly improve those chances. The Cards replied:
1.King of Swords
2.Queen of Cups.
3.Death.
The backgrounds in brief- I met her a few months ago(via a series of startling coincidences btw, not on a dating site), both of us having made clear we were looking for a relationship. Which pretty much started, but then she put the brakes on it, though we did then become very close as friends. Seems she wants some specific changes in my way of being before it will be considered again, but (naturally enough) won't say exactly what they are. I'd love to say it's been a fun challenge, but it hasn't!

Looking at the spread as a whole, the figures don't seem to be communicating with each other directly. Maybe disharmony, maybe simply each (for the want of a better way of putting it) doing their own thing. The KofS is staring coldly out, ignoring the QofC, who's absorbed in gazing at the chalice. While Death marches away in the other direction.

Seems highly significant to me that a King and a Queen were drawn in a love reading, maybe indicating a very important connection, and the possibility/probability of romance. The King and Queen of those suits actually represent our respective characters better than any other would. I am quite verbal, linearly minded, (with a tendency to overthink) and she is highly creative, emotional and intuitive.
The Death Card could show change coming, possibly a change in the nature of our relationship. Or death for one or both of us.
In terms of guidance, the KofS may be saying the presnt situation includes thinking too much on my part, the QofC seems to suggest I could do with being moved more by emotion and intuition, and Death that I should be prepared for big change.
I notice the numbers are closely connected 14 and 13 of their suits and Key XIII, but I have no idea what this might mean.. And so my questions are:

1. Does this sound like I'm on the right track with my interpretation? Is there anything you (or anyone who knows about Tarot and cares to chime in!) would add?

2.The form of 1. Situation 2.Best action 3.likely outcome is one I picked up from a friend but never have seen mentioned by an authority on the subject. Seem serviceable? Think a question was asked about various three card spreads last week actually!
Sometimes the Cards offer me a picture that fit really nicely together and relate to each other in what looks like a kind of circular way, and ignore the request to respond in a neatly delineated fashion. And sometimes they fit perfectly in their "places" and give clear advice! Simply a matter of practice and refinement, or something that needs overhaul?

Many thanks ksh

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) More or less, but I'd read this as saying that the relationship is probably at an end (Death) for reasons that can be understood by exploring the imagery and symbolism of the two face cards. Very often when I get two personality cards like these, it indicates that neither person is going to change.

2) It's an entirely workable scheme.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for that. Disappointing but very interesting. Know this is going to be a good theme for meditation, but can you point to a good place to start with the symbolism of the Personality Cards?

--
